items.q: What makes the antigen-binding site of an antibody specific to one antigen?
items.correct: The variable region — its amino acid sequence (and thus shape) differs between antibodies, complementing a specific antigen.
items.misconception: All antibodies have the same binding site; specificity comes from where they go in the body.
items.random_wrong: Antibodies bind to any antigen they meet first.
items.why: Students miss the role of the variable region in determining specificity, thinking antibodies are interchangeable generic 'germ-killers'.

items.q: Why are enzymes specific to certain substrates?
items.correct: Only substrates with complementary shape and chemistry to the active site can bind effectively.
items.misconception: Each enzyme can react with any substrate, but works fastest with one type.
items.random_wrong: Enzymes choose their substrate based on which one is most common in the cell.
items.why: Students underestimate how strict the lock-and-key/induced-fit relationship is; they think specificity is a preference rather than a structural requirement.
It's Different gene combinations. “Different parents” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: One fertilised egg that splits. “Two eggs” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If a family has 4 children, each inherits half their DNA from how many parents?
items.why: The real figure is 2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
